_meta.purpose: Per-metric provenance overlay on top of the legacy COUNTRIES array baked into index.html. Reader can audit any number by checking source/source_url/as_of/method/quality_flag.

quality_flags.sourced: Verified against a public dataset; source_url + as_of populated.
quality_flags.legacy_curated: Hand-authored heritage value, not yet re-verified. Treated as draft.
quality_flags.modeled: Computed at render time from sourced inputs (e.g., SCE, backcast, f2030 overlay).
quality_flags.manual: Hand-maintained snapshot from an annual public release (e.g., Feeding America).

fields.c: 6-component structural FDRS vector [import_dep, supplier_conc, prod_trend, food_infl, climate, conflict].
fields.w: Wheat caloric share % (CALORIC view — fraction of national caloric supply).
fields.fi: Food inflation baseline % (yr/yr). Live sources override this at render time.
fields.net: Net food trade balance (USD millions, +export -import). Definition: HS chapters 01-24 only.

migration_order: Top food-trade economies: USA, BRA, CHN, IND, RUS, ARG, AUS, CAN, IDN
Major importers: EGY, SAU, NGA, ETH, BGD, PHL, MEX
Crisis countries with active live signals: SDN, YEM, SOM, AFG, HTI, COD, SSD, MMR

w.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Belgium; wheat & products supply ~11% of caloric intake
w.method: Calories from wheat & products / total caloric supply per capita per day
w.note: Wheat IS a meaningful caloric input in Belgium. Domestic winter wheat production fell 41% in 2024 (Statbel) but is non-zero.

r.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Belgium; rice & products contribute roughly 1% of daily calories
r.method: Calories from rice & products / total caloric supply
r.note: Belgium imports ~$374M / 510M kg rice per year (WITS 2024); rice is low-but-nonzero caloric importance.

m.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Belgium; maize products + indirect via animal feed
m.method: Direct caloric supply only; indirect feed conversion not included.

fi.source: Statbel / Trading Economics — Belgium food inflation YoY
fi.method: Latest published HICP food sub-index YoY
fi.note: Live Eurostat HICP read should override at render time. Hardcoded baseline lowered from legacy 10% to current 2.0%.

net.source: ITC TradeMap — Belgium net agri-food trade (HS 01–24)
net.method: Exports (HS 01-24) minus imports (HS 01-24). Excludes processed-food re-exports below HS 24.
net.note: Belgium is a major EU agri-food trader; net balance highly definition-sensitive. FAOSTAT agricultural imports alone are ~$52B in 2024; net of all flows yields +$8B.

w.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Netherlands wheat caloric share
w.method: Calories from wheat & products / total caloric supply
w.note: Dutch diets get less from wheat than Mediterranean Europe; high meat/dairy share lowers the figure.

r.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Netherlands rice caloric share
r.method: Calories from rice & products / total caloric supply
r.note: NL imports ~$481M of rice (OEC 2024) and re-exports ~$320M onward to other EU markets. Caloric contribution is low; trade-import dependency near 100%.

m.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Netherlands direct maize caloric share
m.note: Direct calories only; substantial indirect maize use as feed for the Dutch livestock sector.

fi.source: Statistics Netherlands (CBS) economy dashboard / Trading Economics
fi.method: Latest published food sub-component of HICP, YoY
fi.note: Lowered from legacy 10% to current ~1.8%. Eurostat live read overrides this when available.

w.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Germany wheat caloric share
w.method: Calories from wheat & products / total caloric supply
w.note: Germany is a meaningful wheat producer (top-5 EU); previous c.w:0 was incorrect.

r.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Germany rice caloric share
r.note: Germany imports nearly all consumed rice (over $500M/yr per OEC); caloric share low but non-zero.

m.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Germany direct maize caloric share

fi.source: Destatis / Trading Economics — German food inflation YoY
fi.method: Latest published HICP food sub-index YoY

w.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — France wheat caloric share
w.note: France is the EU's largest wheat producer; wheat-based bread/pasta are dietary staples.

r.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — France rice caloric share

m.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — France direct maize caloric share

fi.source: INSEE / Trading Economics — France food inflation YoY

w.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Italy wheat caloric share
w.note: Pasta and bread dominate Italian carbohydrate intake — wheat caloric share is among the highest in the EU.

r.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Italy rice caloric share
r.note: Italy is the EU's largest rice producer (Piedmont, Lombardy) and a net rice exporter.

m.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Italy direct maize caloric share

fi.source: ISTAT / Trading Economics — Italy food inflation YoY

w.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Spain wheat caloric share

r.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Spain rice caloric share
r.note: Spain is a meaningful rice producer (Valencia, Ebro Delta).

m.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Spain direct maize caloric share

fi.source: INE / Trading Economics — Spain food inflation YoY

w.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Poland wheat caloric share
w.note: Poland is a major EU wheat producer; bread-heavy dietary patterns yield high caloric share.

r.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Poland rice caloric share

m.source: FAOSTAT Food Balance Sheets — Poland direct maize caloric share

fi.source: GUS / Trading Economics — Poland food inflation YoY
KSH / Trading Economics — Hungary food inflation YoY
fi.note: Hungary historically runs higher food inflation than the EU average; verify Eurostat for latest reading.

fi.source: INSSE / Trading Economics — Romania food inflation YoY
CZSO / Trading Economics — Czechia food inflation YoY

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — US wheat caloric share
w.note: US is a top-5 wheat producer; wheat-based bread/baked goods central but US diet is more diversified than EU mean.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — US rice caloric share
r.note: US is a net rice exporter (AR, CA, LA, MS, TX).

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — US direct maize caloric share
m.note: Direct calories only. Indirect maize use via meat/dairy is far larger.

fi.source: BLS CPI Food / Trading Economics

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Brazil wheat caloric share
w.note: Brazil imports most of its wheat (Argentina, Paraguay, Uruguay) despite being agri-export superpower.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Brazil rice caloric share
r.note: Rice is a staple alongside beans. Brazil produces most domestic rice (RS, SC).

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Brazil direct maize caloric share

fi.source: IBGE / Trading Economics — Brazil food-and-beverage inflation YoY

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — China wheat caloric share
w.note: Wheat is the second-largest cereal calorie source after rice; northern China bread-noodle staple.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — China rice caloric share
r.note: Rice is the single largest caloric source in the Chinese diet. China produces most of its own rice.

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — China direct maize caloric share
m.note: Direct calories only; massive indirect use via animal feed.

fi.source: NBS / Trading Economics — China food CPI YoY
fi.note: China food inflation has been near zero / mildly deflationary.

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — India wheat caloric share
w.note: Wheat is the dominant cereal in northern India; rice dominates the south. India is largely self-sufficient on both.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — India rice caloric share
r.note: Rice contributes the largest single caloric block in Indian diet. India is the world's largest rice exporter.

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — India direct maize caloric share

fi.source: MoSPI / Trading Economics — India CFPI Consumer Food Price Index YoY
fi.note: India's CFPI is published monthly by MoSPI and remains elevated relative to EU norms.

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Russia wheat caloric share
w.note: Wheat is the dominant cereal and Russia is the world's #1 wheat exporter.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Russia rice caloric share

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Russia direct maize caloric share

fi.source: Rosstat / Trading Economics — Russia food inflation YoY
fi.note: Russian food inflation elevated post-2022 due to ruble volatility and import substitution costs.

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Argentina wheat caloric share
w.note: Argentina is a top-10 wheat producer and net exporter (mostly to Brazil).

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Argentina rice caloric share

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Argentina direct maize caloric share
m.note: Argentina is a top-4 maize exporter; most export, indirect feed use dominant domestically.

fi.source: INDEC / Trading Economics — Argentina food and non-alcoholic beverages inflation YoY
fi.note: Argentina's chronic high-inflation environment — value is real but volatile. Disinflating from 2024 peaks.

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Australia wheat caloric share
w.note: Australia is a top-10 wheat producer and major exporter to Asia/MENA.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Australia rice caloric share

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Australia direct maize caloric share

fi.source: ABS / Trading Economics — Australia food inflation YoY

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Canada wheat caloric share
w.note: Canada is a top-5 wheat producer/exporter (Prairies).

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Canada rice caloric share

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Canada direct maize caloric share

fi.source: StatCan CPI / Trading Economics — Canada food inflation YoY

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Indonesia wheat caloric share
w.note: Indonesia does not grow wheat; imports ~10Mt/year. Mostly used for instant noodles.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Indonesia rice caloric share
r.note: Rice is by far the dominant caloric source — among the highest globally.

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Indonesia direct maize caloric share
m.note: Maize used both for direct food and feed; Indonesia largely self-sufficient.

fi.source: BPS / Trading Economics — Indonesia food inflation YoY
Statistik Austria / Trading Economics — food inflation YoY

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Egypt wheat caloric share
w.note: Egypt has the highest per-capita wheat consumption globally; ~50-60% imported, primarily from Russia and Ukraine. Wheat is THE strategic staple.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Egypt rice caloric share
r.note: Egypt is a meaningful rice producer (Nile Delta); periodic export bans to manage domestic supply.

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Egypt direct maize caloric share
m.note: Major maize importer; partial domestic production in Nile Valley.

fi.source: CAPMAS / Trading Economics — Egypt food inflation YoY
fi.note: Disinflating from 2023-24 peaks but still elevated; sensitive to EGP devaluation episodes.

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Saudi Arabia wheat caloric share
w.note: Saudi Arabia phased out domestic wheat production by 2016 (water scarcity); ~100% imported.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Saudi Arabia rice caloric share
r.note: Rice (especially basmati from India/Pakistan) is a dietary staple; ~100% imported.

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Saudi Arabia direct maize caloric share

fi.source: GASTAT / Trading Economics — Saudi Arabia food and beverages inflation YoY

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Nigeria wheat caloric share
w.note: Nigeria does not grow wheat at scale; ~100% imported. Bread and noodles increasingly part of urban diet.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Nigeria rice caloric share
r.note: Rice consumption has grown rapidly; partial domestic production, large imports from India and Thailand.

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Nigeria direct maize caloric share
m.note: Maize is a major caloric staple alongside cassava and yams.

fi.source: NBS Nigeria / Trading Economics — Nigeria food inflation YoY
fi.note: Food inflation acute through 2024-26 driven by naira devaluation, fuel-subsidy removal, and insecurity in farming regions.

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Ethiopia wheat caloric share
w.note: Wheat is a meaningful caloric source alongside teff (Ethiopia's domestic staple).

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Ethiopia rice caloric share

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Ethiopia direct maize caloric share
m.note: Maize is one of the largest caloric sources, alongside teff and sorghum.

fi.source: Ethiopia CSA / IMF Article IV staff report
fi.note: Food inflation remains elevated due to ETB devaluation and conflict-affected supply chains.

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Bangladesh wheat caloric share
w.note: Wheat secondary to rice; most imported (Russia, India, Ukraine).

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Bangladesh rice caloric share
r.note: Rice supplies roughly half of all calories — one of the highest single-commodity caloric dependencies globally.

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Bangladesh direct maize caloric share

fi.source: BBS / Trading Economics — Bangladesh food inflation YoY

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Philippines wheat caloric share
w.note: Wheat largely imported (US, Australia); bread and noodles secondary to rice.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Philippines rice caloric share
r.note: Rice is the dominant staple; Philippines is one of the world's largest rice importers (from Vietnam, Thailand).

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Philippines direct maize caloric share

fi.source: PSA / Trading Economics — Philippines food inflation YoY

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Mexico wheat caloric share
w.note: Wheat secondary to maize; partial domestic production, partial imports from US.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Mexico rice caloric share

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Mexico direct maize caloric share
m.note: Maize is Mexico's defining caloric staple (tortillas). Massive direct consumption beyond feed.

fi.source: INEGI / Trading Economics — Mexico food inflation YoY

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Sudan wheat caloric share (pre-war 2022 baseline)
w.note: Wheat heavily imported; conflict-disrupted supply chains since 2023. Verified pre-war FBS baseline; current consumption is much lower per WFP rapid assessments.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Sudan rice caloric share

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Sudan direct maize caloric share
m.note: Sorghum and millet dominate domestic cereals more than maize.

fi.source: IMF / WFP — Sudan inflation Article IV indicators
fi.note: Sudan in active food crisis; IMF estimates triple-digit food inflation. SDG collapse + war disruption. Treat as exceptional, not comparable to peer countries.

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Yemen wheat caloric share
w.note: Wheat is the dominant caloric source. Yemen is ~95% wheat-import-dependent; IPC Phase 3+ exposure is heavily wheat-linked.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Yemen rice caloric share

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Yemen direct maize caloric share

fi.source: WFP HungerMap / IMF Article IV — Yemen
fi.note: Severe food inflation in IRG-controlled areas; Houthi-controlled areas show different price dynamics. WFP HungerMap live overrides this when available.

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Somalia wheat caloric share

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Somalia rice caloric share
r.note: Rice is heavily imported, largely from India and Pakistan; central to urban diets.

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Somalia direct maize caloric share
m.note: Sorghum also a major caloric source; FAO FBS may understate informal cross-border flows.

fi.source: Somalia Central Bank / WFP HungerMap
fi.note: Somalia uses USD widely; nominal food inflation lower than peers, but real food access remains weak due to drought + conflict.

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Afghanistan wheat caloric share
w.note: Wheat is overwhelmingly the dominant caloric source — naan / bread is central to diet. Partial domestic production, large imports from Pakistan and Iran.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Afghanistan rice caloric share

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Afghanistan direct maize caloric share

fi.source: World Bank / WFP — Afghanistan post-2021 deflationary pressure
fi.note: Afghanistan has shown atypical mild deflation post-2021 due to demand collapse and AFN appreciation; real food access remains severely constrained.

w.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Haiti wheat caloric share
w.note: Wheat is ~100% imported (US, EU); bread is urban staple.

r.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Haiti rice caloric share
r.note: Rice is the dominant staple; mostly imported, primarily from the US. Domestic rice production was undercut by liberalization in the 1990s.

m.source: FAOSTAT FBS — Haiti direct maize caloric share

fi.source: IHSI / IMF — Haiti food inflation indicators
fi.note: Gourde devaluation + gang-disrupted supply chains drive elevated food inflation. WFP HungerMap live overrides when available.
